summary: R. Meir explicitly rules the HP's bull is a communal sin-offering (supporting Abaye): it cannot become pigul and overrides Shabbat/tumah. R. Elazar then raises and resolves his dilemma: the Yom Kippur service is exclusively designated (yuchad) - the HP cannot simultaneously fulfill personal votive obligations through it, regardless of whether the bull is classified as communal or personal.

display.whats: R. Meir: the HP's bull is a communal sin-offering
display.hint: R. Meir explicitly states the HP's bull is a chatat tzibur (communal sin-offering), supporting Abaye. This has major practical implications.

learning.summary: R. Meir's explicit ruling: the HP's bull on Yom Kippur is a communal sin-offering. This supports Abaye's position from 50a. Communal sin-offerings cannot become pigul, override Shabbat, and tumah does not disqualify them. R. Meir reads the HP's atonement as extending to all Israel through his representative role, making 'for himself and his household' a description of the HP's representative capacity rather than a limitation.
learning.ahaMoment: R. Meir's communal ruling is the clearest tannaitic statement: the HP represents all Israel, so his bull is communal.
learning.memoryAnchor: R. Meir: HP's bull = communal offering. 'For himself and his household' describes his role, not a limitation.
learning.openQuestion: How can an offering explicitly described as 'for himself' be classified as communal?

argumentFlow.speaker: R. Meir
argumentFlow.text: The HP's bull is a communal sin-offering - he represents all of Israel on Yom Kippur.
argumentFlow.label: Ruling: The HP's bull is a communal sin-offering

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Communal status: the bull cannot become pigul; tumah does not disqualify; Shabbat rules like other communal offerings.
argumentFlow.label: Implication: Communal status: the bull cannot become pigul;

halachic: communal offerings not subject to pigul

misconceptions.misconception: The HP's bull is a personal sin-offering because the HP is atoning for himself and his household, not all Israel.
misconceptions.correction: R. Meir rules the bull is a communal sin-offering. The HP represents all Israel, so his personal atonement has a communal dimension - the bull's legal category follows the representative status of the offerer.

quizSeeds.seed: What does R. Meir rule about the HP's bull?
quizSeeds.answer: It is a communal sin-offering - the HP represents all Israel, making his bull communally classified with all the resulting leniencies (no pigul, overrides Shabbat/tumah).

sugyot.finalRuling: R. Meir: the HP's bull is a communal sin-offering.

display.whats: Can the HP fulfill a personal vow during Yom Kippur service in lieu of a separate offering?
display.hint: R. Elazar raises a dilemma: is the Yom Kippur service exclusively designated, such that the HP cannot simultaneously fulfill personal votive obligations through it?

learning.summary: R. Elazar's question: the HP is bringing his bull on Yom Kippur. He also has a personal vow to bring a sin-offering. Can he intend the Yom Kippur bull to simultaneously fulfill his personal vow? The Gemara rules NO - the Yom Kippur service is exclusively designated (yuchad) for its specific atonement. The HP cannot use it to discharge personal obligations. This is independent of whether the bull is communal or personal.
learning.ahaMoment: The Yom Kippur service is not just important - it is legally exclusive. No personal obligation can be grafted onto it.
learning.memoryAnchor: HP on Yom Kippur: his entire service is designated for national atonement. No personal obligation may share that designation.
learning.openQuestion: Does the exclusive designation of Yom Kippur service prevent the HP from having any secondary personal intent?

argumentFlow.speaker: R. Elazar
argumentFlow.text: Can the HP intend the Yom Kippur service to simultaneously fulfill a personal votive obligation?
argumentFlow.label: Question: Can the HP intend the Yom Kippur

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: No - the Yom Kippur service is exclusively designated (yuchad) for its specific atonement.
argumentFlow.label: Ruling: No - the Yom Kippur service is

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Designated service cannot simultaneously discharge a different obligation - the designation locks the act to its purpose.
argumentFlow.label: Principle: Designated service cannot simultaneously discharge a...

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Like firstborn/tithe: automatic sanctity still requires explicit designation. Yom Kippur's designation works inversely - it locks out all other obligations.
argumentFlow.label: Analogy: Like firstborn/tithe: automatic sanctity still requi...

halachic: HP cannot fulfill personal vows through it

misconceptions: The HP can include personal votive intent in the Yom Kippur service - this is exactly what is prohibited.

visualizableElements.description: Personal vow offering vs. fixed Yom Kippur service: can they coexist in the same act?

quizSeeds.seed: What is R. Elazar's dilemma and the Gemara's resolution?
quizSeeds.answer: R. Elazar asks whether the HP can use the Yom Kippur service to fulfill a personal vow simultaneously. The Gemara rules no: the service is exclusively designated (yuchad) for its specific atonement and cannot discharge other obligations.

sugyot.finalRuling: The Yom Kippur service is exclusively designated - the HP cannot use it to fulfill personal votive obligations.

rashiTranslations.en: R. Meir says: the HP's bull is a communal sin-offering - he represents all of Israel.
Communal sin-offerings cannot become pigul and override Shabbat and tumah prohibitions.
Since it is communal, the blood retains its communal-offering status throughout the service.
Firstborn and tithe animals have automatic inherent sanctity from birth yet still require formal designation.
Automatic sanctity still requires an active designation for use in a specific service.
Similarly, the HP's communal role does not automatically make his bull communal without textual support.
R. Elazar's dilemma: can the HP on Yom Kippur simultaneously fulfill a personal vow or obligation?
The Yom Kippur service is a designated exclusive service - no personal obligations may be incorporated.
The HP's entire Yom Kippur service is consumed by the designated national atonement sequence.
If the HP had a personal vow to bring an offering, he cannot use Yom Kippur service to discharge it.
The designated (yuchad) nature of Yom Kippur service makes it legally exclusive to its specific purpose.
This parallels how a kohen performing one service cannot simultaneously discharge a different obligation.
The principle: an act under specific designation cannot simultaneously satisfy a different obligation.
The HP's vestments on Yom Kippur are also designated - he cannot wear them for personal service.
R. Elazar's conclusion: the Yom Kippur service is completely and exclusively designated.
The yuchad (exclusive designation) principle means designated service is legally separated from all other service.
This protects the integrity of Yom Kippur atonement - entirely for its designated purpose.
A practical case: if the HP had a personal chatat he must fulfill it separately at another time.
He cannot intend to fulfill his personal chatat through the Yom Kippur chatat service.
The Gemara tests this with analogous cases from other designated service contexts.
The principle appears broadly in halacha: designated acts cannot simultaneously discharge other obligations.
A borrowed garment's tzitzit: the wearer does not fulfill his tzitzit obligation because the garment is not his.
Similarly, the HP performing Yom Kippur service 'for himself' cannot use it for personal vows.
The Gemara's conclusion: designated service is exclusive in both directions - cannot be replaced and cannot replace.
This section completes the analysis of the communal/personal bull question and adds the exclusivity principle.
The conclusion: R. Meir says communal; the exclusivity principle applies regardless.
Even if the bull is personal (Rava's view), the HP still cannot incorporate personal vows.
Even if the bull is communal (Abaye/R. Meir's view), the HP cannot use it for personal obligations.
The exclusivity principle operates independently of the communal/personal classification.
R. Elazar's dilemma is resolved: the Yom Kippur service is exclusively designated in both directions.
The HP cannot perform personal service during Yom Kippur; the Yom Kippur service cannot fulfill personal obligations.
This concludes the R. Elazar section; the next daf (51a) turns to blood sprinkling.
The Mishna on 51a: the HP takes blood from the stirrer to enter the Holy of Holies.
Bull blood and goat blood: their respective sprinkling sequences in the Holy of Holies.
The next section details the sprinkling technique and counting formula.
Summary of 50b: R. Meir (communal bull) and R. Elazar (exclusive designation) are the two main contributions.
Both positions reinforce that the Yom Kippur service is legally unique and cannot be supplemented.
The HP's role on Yom Kippur is entirely and exclusively the national atonement service.
No personal element may intrude - neither personal offerings nor personal intent.
This is the theological and halachic core of Yom Kippur's uniqueness in the Temple service.
The next section (51a) describes the physical acts of entering the Holy of Holies with the blood.
Rashi provides the halachic framework for the HP's Yom Kippur obligations in this section.
The designation (yuchad) principle reappears in later sections dealing with incense and goat service.
This concludes Rashi's coverage of the communal/personal and exclusivity discussions on 50b.
The next Rashi entries (51a) deal with the blood reception and entry sequence.
R. Meir's ruling is the clearest tannaitic statement on the HP's bull's communal status.
R. Elazar's dilemma and resolution establish Yom Kippur's legal exclusivity.
Together: the HP's bull is communal AND the service is exclusively designated.
These two principles together define the legal character of the entire Yom Kippur service.
End of Rashi commentary on 50b.
The following material on 51a begins the blood sprinkling sequence.
Rashi's next entries explain the technical aspects of receiving, carrying, and sprinkling blood.
The transition from 50b to 51a marks the shift from legal classification to procedural description.
Summary: communal bull (R. Meir/Abaye) plus exclusive designation (R. Elazar) form the Yom Kippur legal framework.
This framework explains why the HP must perform every act personally and in the correct order.
No delegation, no personal additions, no substitutions - pure designated exclusive service.
Rashi's commentary on 50b covers communal bull, exclusivity, and their combined implications.
The HP on Yom Kippur is the paradigm case of legally designated exclusive service in rabbinic literature.
This explains why so much of tractate Yoma focuses on precise details of his service.
The precision serves the legal requirement of exclusivity - every detail is part of the designated package.
End of 50b Rashi section.
The 51a section begins with the Mishna about blood reception from the stirrer.
The HP receiving blood from the one who was stirring it begins the inner service.
The stirring (meragel) kept blood from congealing; the HP receives it for sprinkling.
This reception begins the most sacred part of the Yom Kippur service.
The sequence of blood services inside the Holy of Holies follows strict halakhic order.
Any deviation invalidates the entire service and requires the HP to repeat it.
